checks.name: tenant and repository authority
checks.summary: Composite PostgreSQL authority, repository preflight, environment-resolved CLI identity, scoped queries, and cross-tenant negative tests prevent repository content or command flags from selecting canonical ownership.

checks.name: committed Git and path boundary
checks.summary: Bounded Git object commands read exact committed blobs; symlinks, submodules, traversal, case collisions, unsupported encodings, oversized content, and mutable-worktree substitutions fail closed.

checks.name: adapter execution and output boundary
checks.summary: Only bundled source-pinned adapters run with bounded requests, output, time, and environment; every result is treated as untrusted and must satisfy schema, source-hash, closure, and deterministic-ID validation.

checks.name: canonical publication and semantic-delta integrity
checks.summary: PostgreSQL recomputes baseline-to-target deltas, constrains previous IDs by entity kind, rejects false rename claims, and atomically binds artifacts, events, outbox rows, and receipts.

checks.name: content-free observability
checks.summary: Metrics and traces are bounded and exclude source bodies, paths, qualified names, tool output, and secrets.

residual_risks: Bundled adapters are trusted release components with resource isolation, not a hostile-code sandbox; repository-provided and third-party adapters remain prohibited.
Unresolved dynamic-language relations remain explicit gaps and may reduce lineage coverage without corrupting canonical certainty.
Git, PostgreSQL, object-store, CI, and host administrators are trusted deployment roles and can alter their respective planes outside application controls.
Production credentials, network policy, encryption keys, backup geography, and object-store capability admission remain deployment responsibilities.
Qdrant and Neo4j projections remain intentionally absent until Sprints 09 and 10.
